schools.summary: Gill: after the laws of the priests' purity and the people's feasts, God spoke of other things concerning both people and priests — the lamps and the shewbread.
Henry: orders for the decent furnishing of the candlestick and table in God's house; the law for the lamps, given before (Exod. 27:20-21), is here repeated as it began to be put in execution.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses
the furnishing of the candlestick and table
the worship of God ordered to its furniture

schools.summary: Gill: Moses, the chief magistrate under God, had authority to command Israel to bring what the Lord required — the pure oil for the light.
Keil & Delitzsch: vv.2-4 — the children of Israel to bring pure beaten olive oil for the light, to cause the lamps to burn continually before the LORD, a statute for ever.

themes: pure oil olive beaten for the light
to cause the lamps to burn continually
the worship sustained by all Israel

schools.summary: Gill: on the outside of the veil that divided the holy from the most holy, before the ark in which the testimony lay.
Ellicott: 'without the vail of the testimony' is the second veil dividing the holy from the most holy; a statute for ever throughout your generations.
Wesley: Aaron was to order it either by himself or by his sons (Exod. 25:37), from evening to morning before the LORD.

themes: Without the vail of the testimony... Aaron order it from the evening unto the morning
a statute for ever
the unceasing light Christ gives his church

schools.summary: Gill, with Jarchi: 'pure' for two reasons — the candlestick was of pure gold, and was to be kept pure and clean, free from ashes, by the priest.

Ellicott: though the candlestick was 'pure' as made of pure gold (Exod. 25:31), the second-temple order was that the priest arranged the lamps after purifying the candlestick and removing the cinders.

themes: He shall order the lamps upon the pure candlestick
pure in gold and in keeping
the church daily purged that her light shine clear

schools.summary: Gill: the fine flour, of wheat, and the finest of it, for the twelve cakes.
Ellicott: in the second temple the wheat, brought as a meat offering, was beaten, ground, and passed through twelve sieves each finer than the last, to make the shewbread.

themes: take fine flour, and bake twelve cakes
the twelve tribes of Israel
Christ the true bread presenting his people

schools.summary: Gill: the twelve cakes set in two rows upon the pure table.
Keil & Delitzsch: vv.6-9 — two rows, six on a row, upon the pure table, with frankincense, renewed every sabbath, and eaten by the priests in the holy place.

themes: set them in two rows, six on a row
upon the pure table before the LORD
the people kept in remembrance before God

schools.summary: Gill: two cups of frankincense, a handful in each, set by each row of the cakes.
Ellicott: the bowls of pure frankincense were placed in the vacant space of the table beside the two piles, the incense burned as the memorial offering by fire.

themes: put pure frankincense upon each row
for a memorial... an offering made by fire
the people's presentation a sweet savour

schools.summary: Gill: describes the manner — four priests bringing in the new rows and frankincense as four bore out the old, the new set as the old was drawn away.
Keil & Delitzsch: every sabbath he set it in order before the LORD continually, taken from the children of Israel by an everlasting covenant.

themes: Every sabbath he shall set it in order
by an everlasting covenant
the fellowship of God and his people kept fresh

schools.summary: Gill: the twelve old cakes were divided between the courses of priests that carried in and brought out, the high priest having half.
Ellicott: in the second temple the cakes were divided between the high priest (six) and the officiating priests (six), eaten in the holy place as most holy.

themes: it shall be Aaron's and his sons'... they shall eat it in the holy place
most holy unto him
the table of the LORD feeding his servants (Matt. 12:4)

schools.summary: Gill: the son of an Israelitish woman whose father was an Egyptian; his name and his mother's are afterward given.

Henry: 'evil manners beget good laws'; the offender was the son of an Egyptian father and an Israelite mother of the tribe of Dan; the Jews say he sought to pitch his tent among the Danites in his mother's right and, being opposed, fell to strife.

themes: the son of an Israelitish woman, whose father was an Egyptian
strove together in the camp
evil manners begetting good laws

schools.summary: Gill: cast in the strife, he fell into outrageous blasphemies against God who made Israel's laws, and cursed the judges who sentenced him; the Jews held he pronounced the ineffable Name itself, his mother being of the tribe of Dan.

themes: blasphemed the name of the LORD, and cursed
brought him unto Moses
the act striking at the honour of God

schools.summary: Gill: he was put in some known prison in the camp, kept in safe custody.

Ellicott: the Mosaic law nowhere enacts imprisonment as a punishment; this was custody till his cause was judged, Moses probably receiving the divine direction in the sanctuary from the mercy seat (Exod. 25:22).

themes: they put him in ward
that the mind of the LORD might be shewed them
Moses waiting on God before sentence

schools.summary: Gill: God spoke from off the mercy seat in the holy of holies, where he had promised to meet and commune with Moses about anything he should inquire.
Ellicott: it is probable Moses received the divine directions in the sanctuary, whether by Urim and Thummim or otherwise, God having promised to reveal his will from the mercy seat (Exod. 25:22).

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses
from the mercy seat
judgment resting on the express word of God

schools.summary: Gill: brought without the camp to show he had no part in Israel; the place of execution was outside, as later without the city.
Keil & Delitzsch: vv.14-16 — let all that heard him lay their hands on his head, and all the congregation stone him.

themes: Bring forth him that hath cursed without the camp
all the congregation stone him
the honour of God the concern of all Israel

schools.summary: Gill: on this occasion God gave Israel laws concerning the affair — whosoever curseth his God shall bear his sin.
Clarke: 'curseth' is he who makes light of God, who does not treat him and sacred things with due reverence, and shall have the guilt imputed to him.
Ellicott: the LORD lays down a general law for the punishment of blasphemers beyond the particular offender.

themes: Whosoever curseth his God shall bear his sin
makes light of God without reverence
the honour of God held sacred

schools.summary: Gill: blasphemy is not the bare uttering of the Name but speaking ill and contemptuously of God with respect to any of his names, titles, perfections, ways, or works.
Clarke: the verb is 'pierces' or 'transfixes' the Name — the Name by which the divine Essence was especially pointed out, to be held peculiarly sacred.

themes: he that blasphemeth the name of the LORD... shall surely be put to death
the stranger, as he that is born in the land
the universal claim of God's honour

schools.summary: Gill: corrects the narrowing of this to Israelites; the original law (Gen. 9:6) respects any man whatever — he that killeth any man shall surely be put to death.
Clarke: blasphemy against God and murder are both punished with death — the blasphemer a curse in society, the murderer forfeiting his life; God requires life for life.

themes: he that killeth any man shall surely be put to death
life going for life
God's image in man the ground of its sanctity

schools.summary: Gill: he must pay the value of the beast, or give another as good in its place.
Keil & Delitzsch: vv.18-21 — he that killeth a beast shall make it good, beast for beast, by restitution.

themes: he that killeth a beast shall make it good; beast for beast
restitution, not death, for the brute
the dignity of human life above the beast

schools.summary: Gill: if a man cause a blemish in his neighbour — does any hurt, mutilation, or deformity by striking — so shall it be done to him. Calvin: the law of retaliation was necessary to restrain the practised villain, who would otherwise break his brother's leg or arm and laugh at the poor man, at God, and at his Law; it is the magistrate's rule, not private revenge.

themes: if a man cause a blemish in his neighbour... so shall it be done to him
the law to restrain cruelty
the magistrate's rule, not private revenge

schools.summary: Gill: records the Jewish reading, that 'eye for eye' is not to be taken strictly or literally but for the price or value of the injury, paid in a pecuniary way.
Keil & Delitzsch: breach for breach, eye for eye, tooth for tooth — the jus talionis, the injury repaid in kind by the magistrate (cf. Exod. 21:23-25).

debates.question: Is 'eye for eye' literal or pecuniary?

sides.position: Literal retaliation administered by the magistrate (the jus talionis)
The price or value of the injury, paid in money

themes: Breach for breach, eye for eye, tooth for tooth
literal retaliation or pecuniary value
exact and equal justice (Matt. 5:38)

schools.summary: Gill: the repetition is for confirmation, though Jarchi distinguishes this (of wounding a beast) from the former (of killing it outright).
Ellicott: the verse repeats the laws enacted in vv.17-18 — restitution for a beast, death for a man.

themes: he that killeth a beast, he shall restore it... he that killeth a man... put to death
the great distinction driven home
the surpassing value of man in God's image

schools.summary: Gill: one manner of law respecting all the foregoing — blasphemy, killing of man or beast, and bodily injury — for stranger and native alike.

Clarke: equal laws, where each individual receives the same protection and privileges, are the boast of a sound political constitution; the person and property of all alike sacred in the sight of justice.

themes: Ye shall have one manner of law, as well for the stranger, as for one of your own country
equal protection for all
the impartiality of God the ground of equal law

schools.summary: Gill: they took the blasphemer, led him out of the camp, laid their hands on him, and stoned him with stones till he died, as the Lord had commanded Moses.
Clarke: the children of Israel did as the LORD commanded, stoning him with stones.

themes: Moses spake to the children of Israel... stone him with stones
the children of Israel did as the LORD commanded
God's honour vindicated in full obedience
